**Brunswick Corporation**, formerly known as the **Brunswick-Balke-Collender Company**, is an American corporation that has been developing, manufacturing and marketing a wide variety of products since 1845. Brunswick has more than 13,000 employees in 24 countries. Brunswick owns a number of brands, including [Mercury Marine](/wiki/Mercury_Marine "Mercury Marine"). In 2024, Brunswick reported sales of [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")5.2 billion. Brunswick's global headquarters is in the northern Chicago suburb of [Mettawa, Illinois](/wiki/Mettawa,_Illinois "Mettawa, Illinois").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunswick_Corporation&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunswick_Corporation&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Brunswick%2C_John_Moses-Portrait_2.jpg/250px-Brunswick%2C_John_Moses-Portrait_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brunswick,_John_Moses-Portrait_2.jpg)

J. M. Brunswick

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Brunswick_Company-Carriage1848.jpg/250px-Brunswick_Company-Carriage1848.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brunswick_Company-Carriage1848.jpg)

Delivery with horse carriage c. late 1800s – early 1900s

Brunswick was founded by [J. M. Brunswick](/wiki/J._M._Brunswick "J. M. Brunswick") who came to the United States from [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") at the age of 15. The **J. M. Brunswick Manufacturing Company** opened for business on September 15, 1845, in [Cincinnati, Ohio](/wiki/Cincinnati,_Ohio "Cincinnati, Ohio"). Originally J. M. Brunswick intended his company to be mainly in the business of making [carriages](/wiki/Carriage "Carriage"), but soon after opening his machine shop, he became fascinated with [billiards](/wiki/Billiards "Billiards") and decided that making billiard tables would be more lucrative, as the better tables then in use in the United States were imported from [England](/wiki/England "England"). Brunswick billiard tables were a commercial success, and the business expanded and opened the first of what would become many branch offices in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") in 1848. It was later renamed **J. M. Brunswick & Brother** by 1860, after a family member came on board, and the company's slogan at this time was: "The oldest and most extensive billiard table manufacturers in the United States".[[3]](#cite_note-JMBB-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/H._W._Collender_Billiard_Manufactory%2C_Stanford%2C_New_York%2C_1870s.jpg/250px-H._W._Collender_Billiard_Manufactory%2C_Stanford%2C_New_York%2C_1870s.jpg)](/wiki/File:H._W._Collender_Billiard_Manufactory,_Stanford,_New_York,_1870s.jpg)

H. W. Collender Billiard Manufactory, Stanford, New York, 1870s

In 1874, the Brunswick company merged with competitor **Great Western Billiard Manufactory** owned by Julius Balke to become the **J. M. Brunswick & Balke Company**. It was incorporated in 1879 with a capital stock of $275,000, the same year it merged with another competitor, **H. W. Collender Company** of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") (founded by Hugh W. Collender), to acquire Collender's patented billiard cushions. In 1884, the partners formed the **Brunswick-Balke-Collender Company** (or **B.B.C. Company** for short).[[4]](#cite_note-Shamos_1999-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Brunswick_Factory_on_State_Street%2C_Chicago_in_1888.jpg/250px-Brunswick_Factory_on_State_Street%2C_Chicago_in_1888.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brunswick_Factory_on_State_Street,_Chicago_in_1888.jpg)

Company warehouse on State Street, Chicago 1888.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Pinsetter_patent_2973204_diagram_excerpt_crop.png/250px-Pinsetter_patent_2973204_diagram_excerpt_crop.png)](/wiki/File:Pinsetter_patent_2973204_diagram_excerpt_crop.png)

[Bowling alley](/wiki/Bowling_alley "Bowling alley") automatic [pinsetter](/wiki/Pinsetter "Pinsetter"), US patent 2973204.

The company expanded into making a number of other products. Large ornate [neo-classical](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism") style bars for [saloons](/wiki/Bar_(establishment) "Bar (establishment)") were a popular product. [Bowling](/wiki/Bowling "Bowling") balls, pins, and equipment led a growing line of [sporting](/wiki/Sport "Sport") equipment. It popularized [bowling balls](/wiki/Bowling_ball "Bowling ball") of manufactured materials, vulcanized [rubber](/wiki/Rubber "Rubber") at first; earlier bowling balls had been solid [wood](/wiki/Wood "Wood").

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunswick_Corporation&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 20th century")]

#### 1900–1949

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunswick_Corporation&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1900–1949")]

In the early 20th century, Brunswick expanded the product line to include such diverse products as [toilet](/wiki/Toilet "Toilet") seats, [automobile](/wiki/Automobile "Automobile") [tires](/wiki/Tire "Tire"), and [phonographs](/wiki/Phonograph "Phonograph").[[5]](#cite_note-5) In the late 1910s, they introduced a quickly popular line of [disc phonograph records](/wiki/Gramophone_record "Gramophone record"), under the name [Brunswick Records](/wiki/Brunswick_Records "Brunswick Records"). In 1930, Brunswick sold the control of the record company to [Warner Brothers](/wiki/Warner_Brothers "Warner Brothers") and came out with a line of [refrigerators](/wiki/Refrigerator "Refrigerator").

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Brunswick-Balke-Collender made small target-drone [aircraft](/wiki/Aircraft "Aircraft") for the [U.S. military](/wiki/U.S._military "U.S. military").
After the war, Brunswick introduced a line of [school](/wiki/School "School") [furniture](/wiki/Furniture "Furniture"). In 1949, the Brunswick "Model A" Mechanical [Pinsetter](/wiki/Pinsetter "Pinsetter") fully automated unit premiered, for the purpose of handling bowling pins for [ten-pin bowling](/wiki/Ten-pin_bowling "Ten-pin bowling"), in competition with [American Machine and Foundry](/wiki/American_Machine_and_Foundry "American Machine and Foundry") (AMF). Previously, Brunswick had made two models of semi-automated, manually operated "spotting tables" for the tenpin sport, that the "Model A" unit replaced.

A historical marker at 623 S. Wabash Ave. in Chicago was dedicated by the [Illinois State Historical Society](/wiki/Illinois_State_Historical_Society "Illinois State Historical Society") in 1995 at the site of Brunswick's headquarters from 1913 to 1964.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The building today is used by [Columbia College Chicago](/wiki/Columbia_College_Chicago "Columbia College Chicago").

#### 1950–1999

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunswick_Corporation&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1950–1999")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Cook_County_Administration_Building_%289181641122%29_%281%29.jpg/250px-Cook_County_Administration_Building_%289181641122%29_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cook_County_Administration_Building_(9181641122)_(1).jpg)

The [Cook County Administration Building](/wiki/Cook_County_Administration_Building "Cook County Administration Building") (originally "Brunswick Building"), where the company relocated its headquarters in the 1960s

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Brunswick_SAW_machine_gun_1974.png/250px-Brunswick_SAW_machine_gun_1974.png)](/wiki/File:Brunswick_SAW_machine_gun_1974.png)

Brunswick [6x45mm SAW](/wiki/6x45mm_SAW "6x45mm SAW") light machine gun, US patent 4066000A.

The 1950s also saw the introduction of a line of [golfing](/wiki/Golf "Golf") equipment to compete with AMF in the leisure-products and sporting-goods markets.

The Brunswick-Balke-Collender Company officially changed its name to the Brunswick Corporation on April 10, 1960. The following year the company reported sales of $422 million. Brunswick acquired Mercury Marine in 1961. In the 1970s, Brunswick introduced the [automatic scorer](/wiki/Automatic_scorer "Automatic scorer"), which electronically tallied the score instead of the bowler doing it by hand.

With the company experiencing substantial revenue growth in the 1950s, it began considering relocating its corporate headquarters.[[7]](#cite_note-New_Office_Skyscraper_Set_For_Loop-7) In the 1960s, it moved its corporate headquarters from the South Wabash Avenue location that it had occupied for six decades to a new [modernist](/wiki/Modernist_architecture "Modernist architecture")-style [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper "Skyscraper") engineered by [Fazlur Khan](/wiki/Fazlur_Khan "Fazlur Khan") of [Skidmore, Owings & Merrill](/wiki/Skidmore,_Owings_%26_Merrill "Skidmore, Owings & Merrill") and developed by [Arthur Rubloff](/wiki/Arthur_Rubloff "Arthur Rubloff")'s firm. The [new Brunswick Building](/wiki/Cook_County_Administration_Building "Cook County Administration Building") was built in the [Chicago Loop](/wiki/Chicago_Loop "Chicago Loop"), next to the new [Chicago Civic Center](/wiki/Chicago_Civic_Center "Chicago Civic Center") (today known as Richard J. Daley Center).[[7]](#cite_note-New_Office_Skyscraper_Set_For_Loop-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Emporis-8) The 1960s-built Brunswick Building was later bought by the [government of Cook County, Illinois](/wiki/Government_of_Cook_County,_Illinois "Government of Cook County, Illinois") in 1996,[[9]](#cite_note-9) and is today known as the "George W. Dunne Cook County Administration Building".[[8]](#cite_note-Emporis-8)

The Brunswick Corporation patented a machine gun using a delayed blowback operation via a fluted chamber as part of the weapon's operation.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Another platform was the [Rifleman's Assault Weapon](/wiki/Rifleman%27s_Assault_Weapon "Rifleman's Assault Weapon"), an unusual grenade launcher that used a spherical rocket propelled grenade.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In the 1980s, Brunswick became a major maker of [yachts](/wiki/Yacht "Yacht") and pleasure [boats](/wiki/Boat "Boat"), under brands including [Bayliner](/wiki/Bayliner "Bayliner"), [Boston Whaler](/wiki/Boston_Whaler "Boston Whaler"), [Maxum](/wiki/Maxum "Maxum"), [Sea Ray](/wiki/Sea_Ray "Sea Ray"), and Trophy.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

During the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War"), Brunswick supplied the military with [camouflage](/wiki/Camouflage "Camouflage") nets. They also made [radomes](/wiki/Radome "Radome") for the [Patriot missile](/wiki/Patriot_missile "Patriot missile").

In 1997, Brunswick purchased the [Roadmaster](/wiki/Roadmaster_(bicycles) "Roadmaster (bicycles)") bicycle division, one of the last U.S. manufacturers of low-cost, mass-market bicycles. However, it became apparent that continued U.S. manufacture of such products was not viable in the presence of surging low-priced imports from overseas producers, primarily [China](/wiki/China "China"). In 1999, Brunswick sold its Roadmaster bicycle division and brand to [Pacific Cycle](/wiki/Pacific_Cycle "Pacific Cycle").

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunswick_Corporation&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 21st century")]

#### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunswick_Corporation&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2000s")]

As of the early 21st century, the Brunswick Corporation still manufactured sporting and fitness equipment ([Life Fitness](/wiki/Life_Fitness "Life Fitness"), Hammer Strength, Parabody) in addition to boats ([Sea Ray](/wiki/Sea_Ray "Sea Ray"), [Bayliner](/wiki/Bayliner "Bayliner"), [Maxum](/wiki/Maxum "Maxum"), etc.) and marine [engines](/wiki/Engine "Engine") under the [Mercury Marine](/wiki/Mercury_Marine "Mercury Marine") [brand name](/wiki/Brand_name "Brand name").

In 2001, Brunswick acquired Hatteras Yachts from Genmar Industries for approximately $80 million in cash.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Brunswick sold its Hatteras/Cabo brand in 2013 to Versa Capital Management.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In 2004, Brunswick acquired [Lowe Boats](/wiki/Lowe_Boats "Lowe Boats"). The same year, the company also purchased Northstar Technologies, a leading marine electronics provider based in Acton, Massachusetts, from Canadian Marconi Corporation (now [CMC Electronics](/wiki/CMC_Electronics "CMC Electronics"), a wholly owned subsidiary of [Esterline Technologies Corporation](/wiki/Esterline "Esterline")). Brunswick then merged Navman, based in Auckland, New Zealand, with Northstar to make Northstar/Navman a supplier to the Brunswick Boat Groups. Brunswick also acquired Mx-Marine. When George Buckley, CEO at the time, left to join [3M](/wiki/3M "3M") in 2006, new leadership decided to sell Northstar, Navman and Mx-Marine to [Navico](/wiki/Navico "Navico"). Brunswick has established regional headquarters in [Verviers](/wiki/Verviers "Verviers"), Belgium; [Monterrey](/wiki/Monterrey "Monterrey"), Mexico; [Dandenong](/wiki/Dandenong,_Victoria "Dandenong, Victoria"), Australia; and [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai "Dubai"), UAE, to better serve its customers by designing, engineering, manufacturing and distributing products based on local needs, using local talent.[[18]](#cite_note-regionalhq-18)

#### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunswick_Corporation&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2010s")]

On July 17, 2014, Brunswick announced plans to leave the bowling business by the end of 2014. The company disclosed that it had agreed to sell its [bowling center business](/wiki/Brunswick_Bowling_%26_Billiards "Brunswick Bowling & Billiards"), which brought in $187 million in revenue in the prior year, to competitor Bowlmor AMF (now known as [Bowlero Corporation](/wiki/Bowlero_Corporation "Bowlero Corporation")) for $270 million. It also disclosed that it had retained [Lazard](/wiki/Lazard "Lazard") to find a buyer for its bowling equipment and products business. The company said it was making these changes to focus on its “core” Marine and Fitness businesses, which provided 92% of company net revenues in 2013. It would retain its heritage billiards business and report billiards financial results as part of the Fitness segment.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The sale of the bowling center business to Bowlmor AMF (Bowlero) was completed in September 2014.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Brunswick completed its exit from the bowling business in May 2015 with the sale of its [bowling equipment and products division](/wiki/Brunswick_Bowling_%26_Billiards "Brunswick Bowling & Billiards") to BlueArc Capital Management, a private investment firm. BlueArc completed the acquisition with investments from Gladstone Investment Corporation, and Capitala Finance Corp. BlueArc continues to produce bowling balls under the Brunswick and DV8 brand names,[[21]](#cite_note-21) and on November 15, 2019, it acquired [Ebonite International](/wiki/Ebonite_International "Ebonite International") and all of its bowling product brands.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In August 2018, Brunswick Corporation acquired Power Products – Global Marine & Mobile business, which includes the global marine, specialty vehicle, mobile, industrial power, and transportation aftermarket products businesses of Power Products, for $910 million in cash from San Francisco-based private equity firm Genstar Capital.

Also in 2018, the company announced it would be separating the fitness business as Life Fitness Holdings in 2019.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In May 2019, Brunswick announced the sale of [Brunswick Billiards](/wiki/Brunswick_Bowling_%26_Billiards "Brunswick Bowling & Billiards"), [Life Fitness](/wiki/Life_Fitness "Life Fitness"), [Cybex](/wiki/Cybex_International "Cybex International"), Hammer Strength, Indoor Cycling Group, and SCIFIT for $490 million to [KPS Capital Partners](/wiki/KPS_Capital_Partners "KPS Capital Partners").[[24]](#cite_note-24) The sale was completed in June 2019.

In May 2019, Brunswick announced it would be purchasing the largest marine franchisor in the United States, [Freedom Boat Club](/wiki/Freedom_Boat_Club "Freedom Boat Club").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Also in 2019, Brunswick announced a new business structure, Advanced Systems Group (ASG) and named Brett Dibkey as president.[[26]](#cite_note-26) ASG is composed of 11 Power Products brands combined with the Attwood Group of businesses including Attwood, Garelick, MotorGuide, and Whale – as well as NAUTIC-ON.

#### 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunswick_Corporation&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2020s")]

On October 4, 2021, Brunswick Corporation announced that it has completed its acquisition of Navico, a manufacturer of marine electronics and sensors for $1.05 billion, adding back to Brunswick the industry sector it had [earlier divested](#2000s).
